sentences.text: Well, it was the beginning of hell.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were all lined up, sick, and old, and people on stretchers out of the hospital.
Everybody had to go to Auschwitz.
We didn't know where we going.
And we were marched-- traffic was stopped and we marched in the-- on the cen-- on the road, in the center of the road.
And the non-Jews were standing around, some smiling, some hiding their smiles.
No one was crying, as I can remember.
Not a morsel of sadness.
I cannot understand why, but it was that, by itself, the heartlessness, was a brutal experience.
And they claimed that they had fears about being punished for showing any kind of sympathy, but I think it goes beyond that.
There was too much hatred, too much ill will for me to allow for any kind of sentimentality.
It was bad, very bad.
I don't remember that.
I just remember the good people and the bad people.
The good people were the ones who were being exiled and tortured, and the bad ones were the ones who were taking over our homes, and were glad to do so, I suppose.
Didn't take that many, because the power behind them was so great that-- we didn't have any guns.
We had no way of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] harming them.
Only they had every way of harming us.
It is-- I don't remember goodness.
I don't know of any family that was hidden by anyone.
Though I-- yes, I should amend that by saying that one family who-- who was sent a great deal of money from America was hidden for dollars.
I don't know for how long.
I don't know if they survived.
But there wasn't a single person that I knew of who was hidden in my town.
And our former customer, a great friend who proclaimed always his friendship and his willingness to help when the-- when the time comes around, when the bell will toll, he was nowhere to be found.
Yes, I have a lot of hard feelings, a lot of bitterness about it.
I know-- I certainly don't know of any help for my family.
None whatsoever.
I understand there were two transports.
I heard the figure of 8,000.
I really don't know, together with the villagers.
But there were two transports, and I don't know if we were in the first or the second.
But we left on May 29, and the reason I know that particularly well, because my birthday is on May 28.
And that was the day we were packing for this big unknown journey, and my mother's eyes were filled with such sorrow.
And every time she looked at her birthday baby, instead of birthday cake, I was given something to pile my clothes in to go on what they called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was that again?
Resettlement, with all its ominous connotations.
And we were taken to the railroad station, and together-- the gendarmes were the lackeys, the helping hand for the Nazis.
And surrounded by, at this time, with enough killing power-- bayonets and-- and-- what are they called-- what are they called?
Rifles?
Rifles, enough to kill 10 times the size of our ghetto.
And then-- then we were piled into the cattle cars.
Non-Jews?
We were so miserable, I don't remember whether people were going-- I know that once we were in the cattle car, as we rode along into the night, people-- some people went crazy.
Some died.
Children were screaming.
There was no toilet.
There was one big [NON-ENGLISH], whatever the translation for a [NON-ENGLISH] is, that was used up in no time at all.
And as the train was jerking, the contents of that big thing were spread all around.
Yeah.
Well, it wasn't a toilet.
What was it called?
[NON-ENGLISH], a huge bin of some kind that filled up in no time at all.
75 To 80 that I heard of, and there was some cars that had later on more.
A few had less, I believe.
It was anywhere between 70 and way over 80.
No.
No room to stand, no room to sit.
My brother kind of laid down on some of that stuff that we took along, and he put some up against the wall of the cattle car for my mother to sit on.
And he laid down kind of in a crouched way, on some of the luggage, and we sat on top of him, several of the sisters.
And I had my-- I was menstruating and there was no way to change my napkin.
It was-- hell was never-- people don't-- it offends even death, yet people were dying all around me.
No.
My brother gave some valuables to the Nazi to pry open the wire.
You see, there was one little window, and it was wired up so that-- it was virtually closed so that no air could enter or escape, and there was nothing but stench and horror.
And he gave the guy some things, but they didn't open it up.
And we didn't think that there is anything worse than that, yet when we arrived in Auschwitz, the--
From Monday afternoon until Wednesday afternoon.
We arrived on May 31st, 1944.

And when we arrived and were confronted with Mengele, and the smoke, and the stench of burning flesh, and the Sonderkommando, and the people who came to empty the cattle cars and threw everybody down.
And my sister and I were looking for our luggage.
We wanted to get it.
And the guy said, what are you looking for?
And we said, for our cigarettes, and she-- and he said-- a young guy, he said-- in the striped clothes-- and he said, you don't need your cigarettes.
Tomorrow you'll be sorry you were ever born.
And we couldn't imagine what he could mean by that.
Can there be anything on the face of the earth worse than the cattle car ride?
And then we found out that that was a cocktail party in comparison to what came after that, when we lived with the stench of burning flesh.
As many as 10,000 to 24,000 people a day were burned.
How did you know that was burning flesh that you were smelling?
Those were were the figures-- how we knew that it was burning?
[LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Even today my kids shield me from a dark smoke if we cross the bridge to go to Jersey or wherever.
Because the smoke was so thick, oozing out of the five chimneys.
And the stench-- do you know what a-- what the stench of a-- when you singe a chicken, as Jews do sometimes?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The singeing, the melting down, the burning. 10,000 to 20,000-- 24,000 human beings a day.
The stench, 50 years later.
You do not know what is lodged in my nostrils.
Unbelievable I can recall the stench of burning flesh from this distance.
I not only can recall it, I cannot not recall it.
And-- and then when the Hungarian Jews were brought in, there was-- there was no room in the crematorium, so they built open fires with all kinds of wood.
And you heard the crackling of the wood, and you saw the flames, and you heard the shrieks.
And they-- I was not right there, but I heard the shrieks, I saw the flames, and they were throwing the children in.
And as long as I live, I will never find out whether my sister was murdered that way, or whether she was taken to the crematorium.
But they were-- trucks, big tractors came around and they lifted it, tilted it, and they tilted the children into the open fire.
And at times, some stronger child might have want to climb out somehow, and Mengele and his cohorts were standing there and pushing them back with a stick.
Mind you, pushing them back with a stick because they wouldn't touch it.
It was dirty work to touch them and push them back.
A stick they pushed them back with, back into the fire.
This is--
My brother saw it, and I saw the flames, I heard the shrieks, I heard the crackling of the wood, in addition to what was oozing out of the crematorium.
But my brother just recently told me that I didn't have children for a number of years.
You know why?
Because I saw it.
Now, I must have been some distance away from it, but, you know, I still see the flames and I hear the crackling, burning of the wood.
And truly, in addition to the swirling, charcoal gray smoke oozing out of the crematoriums.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Human mind can conceive such a thing, somebody can design something like this.
So yes, the man was right-- the young man.
Tomorrow you'll be sorry you were ever born.
And we were taken out of the-- first, we were separated men and women.
You probably heard this over and over again.
Men and women were separated-- OK, let me backtrack.
You got off the train-- you got off the cattle car and somebody separated you?
There was a guard there that separated you?
Not one.
Nazis galore, and--
Dogs.
--And dogs, and rifles, and screamings, and unbelievable noises-- mama, papa.
You might want to read the poem I recently wrote about that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Screaming for their loved ones, children torn away from the arms of parents, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] smoke, smell, thousands of people.
Whoever designed this?
How is it possible?
I can understand that human mind can think of going to the moon.
Going to the Mars, someday to all of the planets, anything I can conceive of.
How-- I cannot conceive of what I saw and lived through there.
I can't believe it even though I was there.
I cannot believe it.
And 50 years later, I don't really only live in America.
No way.
I live in both places, in Auschwitz and in New York City.
There is no way to leave it behind.
It is-- underneath this skin, there is another skin, invisible.
Only I know it, and that skin is Auschwitz.
I am not only here, I always live in two worlds.
I don't know how others feel.
Perhaps they cannot-- they don't want to explain it the same way.
But I-- I live in two worlds forever.
As I am talking to you in a well-appointed apartment in New York City, I cannot get rid of the stench of burning flesh.
I cannot get rid of the sights, the sounds, the smell of Auschwitz.
It is not possible for me.
Help me.
Anyone, help me.
I don't want to be there anymore.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] I can't get away from Auschwitz.
[CRYING]
And mind you, the German government never wants to give me any [GERMAN].
On their last communication with me, after I had sent them my books and all kinds of documents of where I was, articles of all sorts, the German question was, where were you during the war?
And they don't want to give me, at this point, you know, pennies just to feel some kind of power over what happened.
But there is none whatsoever.
Their children are living, [CRYING] and ours perished, and it's not possible for me to not notice the difference.
Their children are not marched off to the crematoriums, and my sister was.
How can you live with this?
I don't know.
I'm trying.
You said you saw Dr.
Mengele when you came into Auschwitz.
How did you know that was him?
I saw him 10 times a day.
We didn't know who Mengele was?
[LAUGHS] The word went through the camp that Mengele is coming to Lager C to select, and we were running for our lives.
We had to run all the time try to hide from him, because we had too much to lose.
We were four sisters then.
In the end, we did lose one more.
And-- and we had to run from his selections.
How  was I not to know who was Irma Grese, who was Mengele?
How was it possible not to know?
How did you lose your younger sister?
He was a very good-looking man.
What?
Yes.
When he-- when he picked us out-- first, they separated the men and the women.
And then, my brother was taken with the men's side, and my-- we stayed too long trying to get the cigarettes, my sister and I.

And by the time we came down, my-- the women and men were selected-- by the time we jumped off the cattle car-- and ran after the group that my mother was going with, to see her, wherever she's going, just to see her.
And we didn't know where she was going.
And just as we reached-- just as I saw the back of her head, Mengele went like this, [GERMAN], these two, my sister and I, and he took us to the life side.
And then they took us into this hideous place, what they called the disinfection, where they stripped us of our clothes, and shaved our head and pubic hair, and threw a rag at us.
If you were that big, you got a garment this small or vice versa.
They just threw a rag at you after they shaved you, within seconds.
Everything had to be so fast and it didn't matter if you were bleeding from the shaving.
Nothing mattered.
And we were in this huge, giant, wet place, and suddenly we looked over there and we saw two monsters that looked familiar, and it was my sister, Regina and Zippy.
Evidently he took them to the crematorium.
He led them to the crematorium group, and at the crematorium he decided to make another selection.
Remember, he was-- he was the ruler of life and death.
He could do anything.
And usually made a ruling for death, not for life.
And he picked out Regina and Zippy, my two sisters.
And by the time we noticed these two monsters, they were shaved in these rags.
And we suddenly yelled, ah, ah, Regina, Zippy.
They're my sisters.
So there were four of us, but a few minutes ago we were a big family of seven.
And then, through the window, somehow this archangel of a human being, my brother, managed to-- he was already shaved and he was given a striped uniform.
And he managed to climb through the window, taking a chance on being shot if they notice him, just so that he can give us one word of encouragement.
And he took the four of us and he said, listen to me, listen.
Eat everything they give you.
If they give you shit, eat shit.
But we must survive.
We have to pay them back.
Not only pay them back, we have to create a better world.
And he disappeared through the window and I never saw him again until I was in America, in 1946, when he arrived in January, 1946.
And beyond this, he would-- he was only in Auschwitz for a few weeks, and he knew that we were in Lager C.
I don't know what lager-- each lager was separated with barbed wire.
You touched it and you burned up.
And he managed to find some pieces of wood as they were building these various blocks, they called, the barracks.
There were some pieces of wood, and he found a knife somehow, and he carved the messages onto these pieces of wood.
Kept saying, you must live, you must live.
The messages were always the same.
And he would throw it to the next camp, and he would beg people to throw it to the next camp, until it finally will reach the Katz girls.
And every day, for about three weeks, we got such a note.
Such the ingenious communicator, you know?
The ultimate communicator.

And we got his messages every day, and then one day there were no more messages, and we were certain that he's either was taken on a transport or he's burned in the crematorium and there is no more Philip.
And then when we came to America, my father got a note from  an American soldier who found him in the hospital with his shot leg.
He was-- he tried to escape, and they shot him.
But he recovered, though I suspect that the bullet might still be in his leg.
And it gives him-- still, 50 years later, it gives him some discomfort.
And all those months in-- in Auschwitz, we no longer had a brother and we were certain we have no mother.

And-- though as certain as we were, the only way I think you can survive is you keep telling each other-- for instance, on Yom Kippur, I remember a conversation of we were-- how we huddled together, the survivor-- we, the people who were still alive, and said, oh, I hope my mother is not fasting.
She couldn't endure it.
We saw the smoke.
We smelled it.
But we tried to kid ourselves by saying that, yes, maybe all the others are dead, but my mother isn't, you know?
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how much time you have left to hear the rest of the story of the escape and liberation.
We can.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you have a job though?
I was going to ask you if you worked in Auschwitz.
In Auschwitz, I met a friend, a girlfriend of a very good friend of mine, who by this time had a job of-- in the Unterkunft, which Unterkunft was the place where you selected the clothing.
And she was able to get seven jobs for seven girls, and I was one of them.
And no-- I was only working there for just a few days when I was-- got terribly ill with typhus, the very typhus that I had until after liberation-- until after I was liberated.
How I survived, I do not know, because a doctor prisoner said to my sisters that, at home, with the best of care, I would die.
And I survived this, but I was young and healthy yet.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after being in Auschwitz and trying to outmaneuver Mengele's selections, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my sister working at the Unter-- actually, I was to work  in the Unterkunft, but because I got so ill, I no longer had the strength to go.
So this girl, at the peril of her life, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] took my sister in in my stead.
She was not supposed to do that.
Any infraction on the holy writ and the holy laws of Hitler was punishable by death, yet she took it upon herself to sneak in my sister, because I had fever perhaps as high as 105.
I don't know how anyone can survive it.
And the only way to quench my thirst standing in the [GERMAN] was that  that little rusty can that some of us had on a string on our-- that was our dish, our-- our china, our porcelain dish.

We-- I took that off, and while I was standing in the [GERMAN] waiting for Mengele to come and-- or Irma Grese, usually, to come and do the counting, the [GERMAN], the roll call, which was the holiest procedure in Auschwitz, and I couldn't bear the  thirst.
And I took off the can, the rusty can, and I urinated into it, and then I drank it.
My thirst was so unbearable.
In any event, when I got ill, my sister got the job and she was able to hold the job, which gave us just a tiny bit of advantage.
We were-- she was able to steal a knife once in a while.
And the poor prisoners would barter their piece of bread for the knife, because then they can slice the knife into several--
--The bread into several slices and were kidding themselves that they have more bread.
They did not, but the slicing meant the difference.
So she would gain a piece of bread for the four of us for a knife.
And whenever she could steal something, she would try stealing and bringing it out-- bring out the knife in her shoes, because they-- otherwise they-- you know, they searched her everywhere.
But she could bring it out in her shoes.
Can you not hear?
